title: BECALMED
byline: Generation ship 'Long Patience'. Reactor cold. No one left but you.

character.backstory: Eighteen years on the watch, the last six standing the reactor vigil on the 'Long Patience' - the one post where you never sleep, because the manual key is an arm's length away and the core does not wait. You know exactly how cold a reactor has to get before it cannot come back, and exactly how long that took. You were asleep for all of it.

watchroom.title: Watch station
watchroom.text:
You wake in the chair you were not supposed to sleep in. One backup cell throws a slow green across the dead consoles, and the green is failing. No drive-hum under your feet. No air-handler. The ship that has carried four generations toward a star none of you will live to see has stopped.

The shift log is open. Your name is at the top of it. The reactor went cold on your watch. The timestamp says nine days ago.

choices.text: Read down the log past your name.
Stand up. Go find the others.

watchroom_log.title: Watch station
watchroom_log.text:
You scroll. The entries after yours are not entries. They are four words, logged by hand, once an hour, by crew who were awake when you were not: he will not wake. Then again. Then a column of it, down and down, in different hands, until the hands stop.

You slept in the one chair on the ship where sleep was forbidden, and the cold came in around you, and no one could rouse you.

choices.text: Close the log. Go find them.

spine.title: Spinal corridor
spine.text:
The corridor runs the length of the ship. Frost has started on the rivets, fine as mold. Your breath hangs in front of you and does not move. The air is becalmed - going nowhere, with you inside it.

Forward, the bridge, where the watch was kept. Aft, the muster hall, where the others would have gathered. Amidships, the lifeboat lock.

sanityText.45: The corridor is longer than it was. You are sure of it now. The frost has crept up the walls in patterns that almost spell four words, and your breath hangs where you left it, marking each place you have already stood. Forward: the bridge. Aft: the muster hall. Amidships: the lifeboat lock.

choices.text: Sit down here in the cold and stop.

bridge.title: Bridge
bridge.text:
Every console is dark. The forward port shows the same handful of stars it has shown for forty years, none of them any nearer. The captain's seat faces the dark, and the captain is in it, hands folded, nine days still.

On the helm lies the manual reactor key - the one the watch turns the instant the core falters. It was an arm's length from your chair. It is an arm's length from you now, and nine days too late to mean anything.

choices.text: Sit in the captain's seat and look at what you let die.
Carry the key down to the reactor deck.

muster.title: Muster hall
muster.text:
The long tables are set. The meal on them is nine days frozen, every plate full, as though everyone rose at once and went somewhere better together. No bodies. Only the places they kept, and the cold keeping them.

A wall locker hangs open. Inside, one med-gel ampoule, the field kind, for a mind that has started to slip its moorings. The rest is bare. The others took what they needed and left you this, or left it by accident; you will not know which. Down the tables, one place is set for one, the chair pulled out. The watch-keeper's. Yours.

choices.text: Walk to your own pulled-out chair and look at it.

lock.title: Lifeboat lock
lock.text:
The cradle is empty. The one boat the ship carried, the one mercy for four hundred, is gone - launched days ago by hands that did the arithmetic and counted you among the lost. They could not bear to wait in here with the cold and the chair and you.

The outer hatch is still here. Manual, a wheel a child could turn, onto the same dark they chose. Frost has reached the spokes. Your hand fits them exactly, and something in you has already decided.

choices.text: Press your face to the inner port and watch where the boat went.
Turn the wheel. Follow them out.

reactor.title: Reactor deck
reactor.text: The core is a black slab gone to absolute cold. The key fits the manual socket the way it always would have, the way it would have nine days ago if you had been awake. You turn it now. Nothing catches. The fuel is past restart, the moderators frozen solid through - you know to the hour when that became true, and the hour was the first one. The key turns and turns in a dead lock, and its small click is the only sound the ship has left. You are turning a key in the cold to feel your own hand move.

choices.text: Keep turning the key until your fingers stop.

ending.text: You turn the key until your fingers stop closing, and go on meaning to turn it after. There is no warmth to make, no core to wake; there never was, after the first hour. The cold takes you the way it took the watch - slowly, while you sit in the chair. Far forward, the last green cell gives out, and the ship keeps its long patience with you at last.

The wheel gives easily. The hatch breaks its seal, and the dark takes the air in one soft pull and then takes you. For a single clean moment before the cold there is no chair, no log, no four words - only the emptiness the others chose, and you going out into it after them. Too late, and willing, and alone.

You sit down in the corridor where the frost spells four words, and you do not get up. There is nowhere the getting up would take you. The ship holds still around you, becalmed, and you keep its last watch the way you kept its first - sitting down - until the cold closes the log on you too, and the green cell forward winks out, and the ship is finally, fully, alone.
